properties.context: Watson visits Holmes and accompanies him throughout the Blue Carbuncle investigation
Watson is the first-person narrator of the Adventure of the Blue Carbuncle
Peterson brought the hat and goose to Holmes on Christmas morning; later returned to show the blue carbuncle found in the crop
Peterson witnessed the altercation and found the goose and hat
Holmes recovered the blue carbuncle and notified the Countess
Holmes deduced Henry Baker's circumstances from his hat before meeting him
Henry Baker was a member of the Alpha Inn goose club
Henry Baker was carrying the Christmas goose when assaulted
James Ryder stole the blue carbuncle with Catherine Cusack's help
Catherine Cusack conspired with Ryder to steal the carbuncle and frame Horner
John Horner was falsely accused of the theft; framed by Ryder and Cusack
The blue carbuncle is the property of the Countess of Morcar
The Countess of Morcar was staying at the Hotel Cosmopolitan when the carbuncle was stolen
Mrs. Oakshott is James Ryder's sister
Ryder hid the blue carbuncle by forcing it down the throat of a goose at Mrs. Oakshott's yard
Mrs. Oakshott sold 24 geese on December 22nd to Breckinridge at Covent Garden
Breckinridge sold two dozen geese to Windigate of the Alpha Inn
Windigate's Alpha Inn goose club supplied Henry Baker with his Christmas goose
Holmes confronted James Ryder with the truth about the carbuncle theft and released him on humanitarian grounds
Holmes released James Ryder without pressing charges at Christmas, reasoning the case against Horner would collapse
The blue carbuncle was found in the banks of the Amoy River in southern China
Watson is staying at Baker Street and companioning Holmes during the Five Orange Pips case
Watson is the first-person narrator of the Five Orange Pips case
John Openshaw came to Holmes for help regarding the KKK threat to his family
KKK sent five orange pips from Pondicherry to Colonel Openshaw; he was subsequently murdered
KKK sent five orange pips from Dundee to Joseph Openshaw; he was subsequently murdered
KKK sent five orange pips from East London to John Openshaw; he was subsequently murdered
Captain James Calhoun was identified by Holmes as the leader of the KKK gang
Holmes identified Captain Calhoun as the leader of the KKK gang through Lloyd's registers and port records
Holmes sent five orange pips to Captain Calhoun at Savannah as a warning

Colonel Openshaw owned the brass box with KKK documents; burned the papers before his death
John Openshaw provided the surviving diary page to Holmes
Major Prendergast advised John Openshaw to consult Holmes
Major Freebody was an old friend of Joseph Openshaw; Joseph was visiting him when he died
Fordham the Horsham lawyer was summoned by Colonel Openshaw to witness his will

Lone Star was at Dundee in January 1885
Lone Star arrived at Albert Dock, London the week before John Openshaw's murder
Colonel Elias Openshaw took a small estate near Horsham in Sussex around 1869-70
John Openshaw lived near Horsham after inheriting the property
Watson is Holmes's long-term friend and companion; assists in the fire alarm operation
Watson is the first-person narrator of A Scandal in Bohemia
Watson threw the smoke-rocket and raised the cry of fire as instructed by Holmes
The King of Bohemia hired Holmes to recover the compromising photograph from Irene Adler
Holmes holds Irene Adler in unique intellectual admiration as the only woman who outwitted him
The King had a past romantic entanglement with Irene Adler in Warsaw; wrote compromising letters and was photographed with her
properties.timeframe: approximately five years before the story

properties.context: The King is engaged to marry Clotilde Lothman von Saxe-Meningen
The King belongs to the hereditary House of Ormstein, kings of Bohemia

properties.date: Day before Holmes's fire alarm operation
properties.location: Church of St. Monica, Edgeware Road

properties.context: Holmes inadvertently served as legal witness to Irene Adler and Godfrey Norton's marriage
Holmes devised the fire alarm plan to trick Irene Adler into revealing the photograph's location
Irene Adler held the compromising photograph hidden behind a sliding panel at Briony Lodge; kept it for self-protection
properties.hiding_place: recess behind sliding panel above right bell-pull at Briony Lodge

properties.context: Irene Adler wrote the letter to Holmes revealing her awareness of his plan and her decision to flee
Irene Adler and Godfrey Norton left England from Charing Cross by the 5:15 train to the Continent
The King (via agent) sent the anonymous letter to Holmes requesting consultation
The anonymous letter was written on Bohemian paper from Egria
Watson accompanies Holmes throughout the Red-Headed League investigation
Watson is the first-person narrator of the Red-Headed League case
Jabez Wilson approached Holmes to investigate the Red-Headed League
Wilson was recruited as a member of the Red-Headed League and paid £4/week to copy the Encyclopædia Britannica
Wilson runs his pawnbroker's business at Saxe-Coburg Square
Wilson is a Freemason, identified by Holmes from his arc-and-compass breastpin
Vincent Spaulding (John Clay) manipulated Wilson into applying for the Red-Headed League to keep him away from his pawnshop
John Clay co-created the fraudulent Red-Headed League scheme to distract Wilson and enable the tunnel robbery
John Clay spent months digging the tunnel from Wilson's cellar to the City and Suburban Bank vault
John Clay and Archie worked together on the tunnel and attempted bank robbery
John Clay was apprehended by Holmes in the City and Suburban Bank vault
Duncan Ross managed the fraudulent Red-Headed League office at Pope's Court
Duncan Ross (William Morris) was an associate of John Clay in the scheme
Duncan Ross ran the Red-Headed League office at 7 Pope's Court, Fleet Street
Peter Jones is an official police agent from Scotland Yard
Peter Jones participated in the bank vault ambush alongside Holmes, Watson and Merryweather
Merryweather is chairman of directors of the City and Suburban Bank
Merryweather accompanied Holmes and Watson in the bank vault ambush
Holmes planned and led the bank vault ambush resulting in John Clay's arrest
Holmes investigated the planned robbery of the City and Suburban Bank
City and Suburban Bank borrowed French gold from the Bank of France
The tunnel was dug to access the City and Suburban Bank vault and steal the French gold
Holmes identified Vincent Spaulding as John Clay from physical descriptions and prior knowledge
Holmes attended Sarasate's concert at St. James's Hall during the investigation
Watson accompanied Holmes to Sarasate's concert
Holmes called upon Scotland Yard after the concert to arrange the bank vault operation

The Red-Headed League was supposedly founded by the fictional Ezekiah Hopkins
Wilson was paid to copy the Encyclopædia Britannica as his nominal duty in the Red-Headed League

Gustave Flaubert wrote to George Sand the quote referenced by Holmes
Watson accompanies Holmes in the Speckled Band investigation including the night vigil

Helen Stoner came to Holmes fearing for her life and asked him to investigate
Dr. Roylott married Mrs. Stoner (widow of Major-General Stoner) in India
Helen Stoner is engaged to Percy Armitage; this engagement was the trigger that moved Helen into Julia's room
Julia Stoner became engaged to a half-pay major of marines; their impending wedding was the trigger for Roylott's murder plan
Dr. Roylott murdered Julia Stoner using the swamp adder passed through the ventilator
Dr. Roylott was killed by his own swamp adder after Holmes drove it back through the ventilator
The swamp adder was the murder weapon used to kill Julia Stoner
The swamp adder fatally bit Dr. Roylott after being driven back through the ventilator
Dr. Roylott trained and used the swamp adder as a murder weapon against his stepdaughters
Holmes prevented a second murder of Helen Stoner by striking the snake with his cane
The will showed that each daughter's marriage would cost Roylott £250/year; this was his financial motive for murder
Holmes consulted the will at Doctors' Commons to establish financial motive
Mrs. Farintosh told Helen Stoner about Holmes
Holmes and Watson stayed at the Crown Inn during their night investigation at Stoke Moran
The dummy bell-rope was fastened above the ventilator to serve as a bridge for the snake
The ventilator opened between Roylott's room and the adjacent bedroom
Watson shares rooms with Holmes and accompanies him to Winchester and the Copper Beeches
Watson is the first-person narrator of the Adventure of the Copper Beeches
Violet Hunter wrote to Holmes for advice about the governess position and later summoned him when in danger
Violet Hunter was hired to impersonate Alice Rucastle without knowing the full situation
Jephro Rucastle is Alice's father from his first marriage
Rucastle's second wife; they have been married about seven years
Edward is Rucastle's young son by his second wife
Rucastle imprisoned his daughter Alice to control her inheritance and prevent her marriage
Fowler was Alice's fiancé; stuck by her even after her brain-fever; married her by special licence in Southampton
Fowler arranged Alice's escape through the skylight using a ladder, aided by the bribed Tollers
Fowler bribed Toller with drink money so he would be incapacitated on the night of Alice's escape
Mrs. Toller helped Fowler's plan in exchange for his generosity
Toller is the only person who can handle Carlo and lets him loose each night
Carlo attacked and mauled Rucastle when Rucastle returned to deal with Holmes and Watson
Watson shot Carlo to stop the attack on Rucastle
Holmes deduced the full scheme of Alice's imprisonment and Violet's role in the imposture
Violet Hunter was registered at Westaway's agency looking for a governess position

Alice Rucastle and Mr. Fowler married by special licence in Southampton the day after their flight
Violet Hunter found the coil of chestnut hair matching her own in a locked drawer in her room
Violet Hunter discovered the locked wing and the barricaded central door while the Tollers were incapacitated

Watson shares rooms with Holmes and accompanies him to Fairbank
Watson is the first-person narrator of the Adventure of the Beryl Coronet
Alexander Holder came to Holmes in desperation to recover the missing beryls and exonerate his son
Mary is Alexander's niece; adopted as daughter after her father died five years ago
Arthur and Mary are cousins; Arthur was in love with Mary and twice proposed to her
Sir George Burnwell was Arthur's friend who had a negative influence over him
Sir George Burnwell had a secret understanding with Mary Holder and fled with her
Mary Holder stole the coronet and passed it out the window to Sir George Burnwell
Sir George Burnwell received the stolen coronet and kept the three gems after the struggle
Arthur saw Mary take the coronet, pursued Sir George Burnwell and struggled with him to recover it
Holmes reconstructed the events of the coronet theft from snow tracks and deduction
Holmes bought back the three beryls from the receiver for £1,000 each after confronting Burnwell
The noble client deposited the Beryl Coronet as security for a £50,000 loan
Lucy Parr went out secretly at night to see Francis Prosper, her sweetheart
Mary Holder stole the coronet from Alexander's bureau and passed it out the window
Arthur recovered most of the coronet from Burnwell in the struggle; brought back 36 of the 39 gems

Watson is the first-person narrator of the Adventure of the Noble Bachelor
Lord St. Simon consulted Holmes about his wife's disappearance
Lord St. Simon married Hatty Doran at St. George's, Hanover Square
properties.status: marriage effectively void as Hatty was already married to Moulton

properties.context: Hatty Doran had secretly married Frank Moulton in San Francisco in 1884; their marriage was her true first marriage
Lord St. Simon had a long intimate friendship with Flora Millar who had been a dancer at the Allegro
Lestrade had Flora Millar arrested believing she was implicated in Lady St. Simon's disappearance
Holmes determined that Hatty had fled to her first husband Frank Moulton and located them at Gordon Square
Holmes used the hotel bill fragment to trace Frank Moulton to Northumberland Avenue hotel and then to Gordon Square

Lestrade had no objection to Holmes's co-operation
Alice had known Frank Moulton in California and was always his friend

Frank and Hatty Moulton were found by Holmes at their lodgings at 226 Gordon Square
Watson accompanies Holmes in the Man with the Twisted Lip investigation after encountering him at the opium den

Kate Whitney and Mrs. Watson are old school companions
Isa Whitney was found by Watson in the Bar of Gold opium den after two days absence
Watson retrieved Isa Whitney from the opium den and sent him home in a cab
Holmes disguised himself as an elderly opium addict to gather intelligence in the Bar of Gold
Neville St. Clair disguised himself as the beggar Hugh Boone using stage makeup skills
Mrs. St. Clair had Holmes investigating her husband's disappearance and provided accommodation at The Cedars

Mrs. St. Clair saw her husband at the second-floor window of the opium den before he disappeared

Hugh Boone was remanded to Bow Street police station cells

St. Clair slipped his signet ring to the Lascar to pass on with his letter to his wife
Mrs. St. Clair received the letter from her husband the day Holmes visited
As Hugh Boone, Neville St. Clair had his regular begging spot in an angle of the wall on Threadneedle Street
Watson accompanies Holmes to Boscombe Valley at Holmes's telegraphed request
Watson is the first-person narrator of the Boscombe Valley Mystery
Alice Turner retained Holmes to prove James McCarthy innocent
Lestrade was retained by Alice Turner to work out the case in James McCarthy's interest
Lestrade, puzzled by the case, referred it to Holmes
John Turner struck Charles McCarthy with a jagged stone at Boscombe Pool on June 3rd
Charles McCarthy blackmailed John Turner for approximately 20 years after recognising him as Black Jack of Ballarat
John Turner gave Charles McCarthy Hatherley Farm rent-free under blackmail pressure
James McCarthy secretly married a Bristol barmaid at a registry office two years before the events of the case
James McCarthy is madly in love with Alice Turner but could not marry her due to his secret marriage
Charles McCarthy insisted his son James should marry Alice Turner as part of his blackmail of Turner
William Crowder witnessed Charles McCarthy walking to Boscombe Pool and James McCarthy following him
Patience Moran witnessed the quarrel between Charles and James McCarthy near Boscombe Pool
Holmes identified John Turner as the murderer through deductions from footprints, cigar ash, cloak and dying words
Holmes worked to prove James McCarthy innocent and submitted objections that led to his acquittal
John Turner was the leader of the Ballarat Gang known as Black Jack of Ballarat
Charles McCarthy was the wagon driver of the gold convoy attacked by the Ballarat Gang
Holmes wrote down Turner's confession to be used only if McCarthy was condemned
John Turner confessed to Holmes and Watson
Watson witnessed Turner's signed confession
Boscombe Pool is situated at the boundary between Hatherley Farm and Turner's estate
Watson accompanies Holmes in the Engineer's Thumb investigation
Watson is the first-person narrator and also introduced this case to Holmes
Watson treated Hatherley's severed thumb wound
Hatherley came to Holmes after being introduced by Watson
Colonel Stark hired Hatherley under false pretences for 50 guineas to inspect the hydraulic press
Colonel Stark severed Hatherley's thumb with a cleaver as he escaped from the window
Elise warned Hatherley to leave, opened the secret panel to save him from the press, and guided him to the window
Elise lived at the house and was associated with the gang
The gang operated the hydraulic press to produce counterfeit coins
The counterfeiting gang operated from the house at approximately six miles from Eyford
Holmes identified the gang as counterfeiters using the hydraulic press to make amalgam coins
Inspector Bradstreet accompanied Holmes, Watson and Hatherley to Eyford
Holmes deduced that Jeremiah Hayling, a previous hydraulic engineer, was murdered by Stark when he previously serviced the press
The fire started from Hatherley's crushed oil-lamp burned the counterfeiting house to the ground
The Paddington guard escorted Hatherley to Watson's consulting room
Watson shares rooms with Holmes and accompanies him during the Case of Identity investigation
Watson is the first-person narrator of the Case of Identity
Mary Sutherland came to Holmes for help regarding the disappearance of Hosmer Angel
Mary Sutherland was engaged to the fictitious Hosmer Angel (James Windibank in disguise)
Uncle Ned in Auckland left Mary £2,500 in New Zealand stock
James Windibank disguised himself as Hosmer Angel using tinted glasses, moustache, bushy whiskers and a whisper
James Windibank married Mary's mother, a woman much older than himself
Windibank disguised himself as Hosmer Angel to keep Mary emotionally bound and prevent her from marrying and losing her income
Mary's mother was complicit in the Hosmer Angel deception with Windibank
Holmes exposed James Windibank as Hosmer Angel by matching typewriter defects
Holmes analysed the typewriter used by Windibank and matched its defects to the letters sent as Hosmer Angel
Mary Sutherland placed a missing person advertisement in the Chronicle for Hosmer Angel

Holmes analysed the typewritten letters from Hosmer Angel to identify the typewriter

Hosmer Angel disappeared from a four-wheeler cab on the morning of the planned wedding

Mary Sutherland came to Holmes after hearing about him from Mrs. Etherege
